annotations.label: Laplace transform of f(t)

anchor.snippet: The Laplace transform of a function f ( t ) , defined for all real numbers

annotations.note: No Laplace transform of a function exists; complexMGF (∫ cexp(z·X) dμ) is the analogous measure-based exponential integral transform.

anchor.snippet: One can define the Laplace transform of a finite Borel measure

annotations.note: complexMGF id μ z = ∫ cexp(z·x) dμ is exactly the (bilateral) Laplace transform of a finite measure, up to the sign convention in the exponent.

anchor.snippet: An important special case is where

annotations.note: The Laplace transform of a probability measure is the moment-generating function mgf X μ t = μ[exp(t·X)], with Dirac measures available as MeasureTheory.Measure.dirac.

annotations.label: Bilateral Laplace transform

anchor.snippet: The bilateral Laplace transform F ( s ) is defined as follows

annotations.note: complexMGF integrates cexp(z·X) over the whole measure space, matching the two-sided Laplace transform of the distribution.

annotations.label: Uniqueness up to measure zero

anchor.snippet: Two integrable functions have the same Laplace transform only if they differ on a set of Lebesgue measure zero

annotations.note: Mathlib proves complex MGFs (the measure Laplace transform) separate distributions, an injectivity analog, but not the a.e.-equality statement for integrable functions.

annotations.label: Inverse Laplace transform (Bromwich integral)

anchor.snippet: The inverse Laplace transform is given by the following complex integral

annotations.note: No Bromwich/inverse-Laplace integral exists; mellinInv is the analogous vertical-line complex contour inversion integral.

annotations.label: Laplace transform as expectation

anchor.snippet: If X is a random variable with probability density function f

annotations.note: mgf X μ t = μ[exp(t·X)] = E[e^{tX}] is exactly the Laplace transform written as an expectation (up to the s ↦ -t sign convention).

annotations.note: The purely algebraic/operational construction of the Laplace transform is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abscissa of absolute convergence

anchor.snippet: The set of values for which F ( s ) converges absolutely is either of the form

annotations.note: convex_integrableExpSet shows the convergence set is an interval (half-line), the analog of the abscissa of convergence determining a half-plane.

annotations.label: Analyticity in region of absolute convergence

anchor.snippet: The Laplace transform is analytic in the region of absolute convergence

annotations.note: analyticOn_complexMGF proves the complex MGF (measure Laplace transform) is analytic on the vertical strip of convergence, the exact analyticity-in-ROC statement for measures.

annotations.label: Convergence extends to larger half-plane

anchor.snippet: then it automatically converges for all s with

annotations.note: Convexity of integrableExpSet encodes that convergence at one exponent extends across the interval, analogous to the half-plane extension.

anchor.snippet: the Laplace transform of f can be expressed by integrating by parts

annotations.note: The integration-by-parts representation of the Laplace transform is not formalized.

annotations.label: One-to-one correspondence with holomorphic functions

anchor.snippet: the Laplace transform gives a one-to-one correspondence between the holomorphic functions

annotations.note: The bijection between Laplace transforms and holomorphic functions with growth bounds is not formalized.

annotations.note: BIBO stability criteria for LTI systems are not formalized in Mathlib.
